message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

content.text:
I am part of a team doing achallenge to build AI tools to help with investigative research.

I want to adapt a process for building research findings that I have used in other contexts. The process files, inputs, and outputs for that process are in C:\Users\rcalv\Projects\Northwestern Project\Sample Process

The competition description for the outcomes we are looking for is here: 
https://www.gain-agent-challenge.northwestern.edu/details/

I have the data maual for the data that will be in the competition here: C:\Users\rcalv\Projects\Northwestern Project\data_manual.md

I am still downloading the raw data.

First please review the competition description and the data file, then review the claude.md file and the various workflow files in the sample process folder. 

Then lets make a plan for analysing the data in the competition.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Data Manual
2	
3	This dataset is stored under `data/`. Together the files cover congressional communications and the lobbying activity aimed at Congress from 2022 through Q1 2026. This manual describes the current on-disk layout, how to load the data, and where to start digging.
4	
5	## Data layout
6	
7	The `data/` directory is decompressed.
8	
9	```
10	data/
11	  congress_press/
12	    2022/2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
13	    2023/2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
14	    2024/2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
15	    2025/2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
16	    2026-01.jsonl
17	    2026-02.jsonl
18	    2026-03.jsonl
19	  senate/
20	    2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
21	    constants/
22	  house/
23	    2022_Registrations_XML/
24	    2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
25	    2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
26	    2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
27	    2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
28	    2026_Registrations_XML/
29	    2026_1stQuarter_XML/
30	```
31	
32	## At a glance
33	
34	| Dataset | Path | Format | Coverage | On-disk size |
35	|---|---|---|---|---|
36	| Congress press releases | `data/congress_press/` | JSONL (51 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026-03 | 504 MB |
37	| Senate LDA filings & contributions | `data/senate/` | JSON (arrays, nested) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 2.2 GB |
38	| House LDA registrations & reports | `data/house/` | XML (409,650 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 5.9 GB |
39	
40	All three are public records. Expect self-reported data, missing fields, and inconsistent conventions — part of the investigative work is reconciling them.
41	
42	---
43	
44	## 1. Congress press releases — `data/congress_press/`
45	
46	**Description.** Press releases scraped from the official `*.house.gov` and `*.senate.gov` member websites. Each record is one release with light member metadata and the full body text.
47	
48	**Scale.** ~48K releases in 2025 alone (≈478 members represented); the full 2022–2026-Q1 corpus runs larger. Average release ~900 words → tens of millions of words of unstructured text.
49	
50	**Format.** Newline-delimited JSON (JSONL). One record per line. The full years 2022 through 2025 are split into year directories; the 2026 Q1 files sit directly in `data/congress_press/`.
51	
52	```
53	data/congress_press/
54	  2022/
55	    2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
56	  2023/
57	    2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
58	  2024/
59	    2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
60	  2025/
61	    2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
62	  2026-01.jsonl
63	  2026-02.jsonl
64	  2026-03.jsonl
65	```
66	
67	**Record fields.** `url`, `title`, `date`, `date_source`, `source` (member press-release index page), `domain`, `scraper` (scraper ID), `member` (`bioguide_id`, `name`, `party`, `state`, `chamber`), `text` (full body, newline-preserved).
68	
69	**Source.** [Congress Press](https://thescoop.org/congress-press/)
70	
71	**Timeframe.** 2022-01 through 2026-03. The 2026 files currently include January through March at the root of `data/congress_press/`.
72	
73	**Load it.**
74	
75	```python
76	import json
77	from pathlib import Path
78	
79	root = Path("data/congress_press")
80	
81	# Single month
82	with open(root / "2026-01.jsonl") as f:
83	    for line in f:
84	        rec = json.loads(line)
85	        print(rec["member"]["name"], rec["title"])
86	
87	# Full decompressed year
88	for path in sorted((root / "2025").glob("*.jsonl")):
89	    with open(path) as f:
90	        for line in f:
91	            rec = json.loads(line)
92	            # ...
93	```
94	
95	**Starting points.**
96	- Keyword and frequency analysis: who talks about a given issue, how often, and when does the language shift.
97	- Comparative messaging: same event, different party framing — pull all releases from a +/- 7-day window around a news event and cluster.
98	- Member activity baselines: releases/month per member; outliers often correlate with primary campaigns, committee news, or scandal cycles.
99	- Entity extraction: NER over `text` to pull companies, agencies, and bills — the output bridges naturally to the lobbying datasets via `bioguide_id` and entity names.
100	
101	---
102	
103	## 2. Senate LDA data — `data/senate/`
104	
105	**Description.** Lobbying Disclosure Act filings submitted to the Secretary of the Senate: registrations, quarterly activity reports, and semiannual contribution (LD-203) reports. This is the "who is lobbying whom, on what, for how much" dataset.
106	
107	**Scale (2025).** 108,225 filings, 39,438 contribution reports (with ~149K line items across them), 5,470 registrants, 28,246 clients. Earlier years are comparable.
108	
109	**Format.** JSON arrays, deeply nested. Each filing includes its registrant, client, lobbying activities (with ALI issue codes and lobbyists), and government entities lobbied. Contribution reports include a `pacs` and `contribution_items` list.
110	
111	```
112	data/senate/
113	  2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
114	    filings/filings_{year}.json          # array of filing objects
115	    contributions/contributions_{year}.json
116	  constants/
117	    countries.json
118	    filing_types.json                    # RR, Q1..Q4, MM (mid-year), YE (year-end), etc.
119	    government_entities.json             # agencies/chambers lobbied
120	    lobbying_activity_issues.json        # 3-letter ALI codes (BUD, TAX, HCR, …)
121	    states.json
122	    lobbyist_prefixes.json
123	    lobbyist_suffixes.json
124	    contribution_item_types.json
125	```
126	
127	**Key fields.** Filing: `filing_uuid`, `filing_type`, `filing_period`, `filing_year`, `income`, `expenses`, `registrant` (nested), `client` (nested), `lobbying_activities[]` (each with `general_issue_code`, `description`, `lobbyists[]`, `government_entities[]`). Contributions: `filing_uuid`, `filer_type`, `registrant`, `lobbyist`, `pacs[]`, `contribution_items[]` (with `type`, `amount`, `payee`, `honoree`, `contributor_name`).
128	
129	**Source.** [Senate LDA API](https://lda.senate.gov/api/v1/) (`filings`, `contributions` endpoints).
130	
131	**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 is Q1 only).
132	
133	**Load it.**
134	
135	```python
136	import json
137	
138	with open("data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json") as f:
139	    filings = json.load(f)           # list of dicts
140	
141	print(len(filings), "filings")
142	f0 = filings[0]
143	print(f0["registrant"]["name"], "→", f0["client"]["name"])
144	for act in f0["lobbying_activities"]:
145	    print(" ", act["general_issue_code"], act["description"][:80])
146	
147	# Decode issue codes using the constants file
148	issues = {c["value"]: c["name"]
149	          for c in json.load(open("data/senate/constants/lobbying_activity_issues.json"))}
150	```
151	
152	**Starting points.**
153	- Top-of-funnel aggregates: biggest spenders by client, most-active registrants, busiest issue codes per quarter.
154	- Revolving door: the `covered_position` field on each lobbyist flags prior government roles — mine it for text patterns ("Chief of Staff to Sen. …").
155	- Foreign influence: filter on `foreign_entities[]` across filings; cross-reference with FARA.
156	- Contribution flows: `contribution_items[].payee` vs. `honoree` reveals who lobbyists and their PACs are funding. Join to FEC for validation.
157	- Data-quality stories: Many filings lack income data, expenses, and state info. The gaps are themselves reportable.
158	
159	---
160	
161	## 3. House LDA data — `data/house/`
162	
163	**Description.** The House Clerk's parallel set of LDA disclosures. Registrations (LD-1) and quarterly activity reports (LD-2). Same underlying disclosure regime as the Senate data, different distribution format and slightly different schema.
164	
165	**Scale (2025).** ~7,762 registrations + ~100,760 quarterly records ≈ 108K records, comparable to the Senate side. The full extracted House tree contains 409,650 XML files.
166	
167	**Format.** One XML document per filing. Each reporting period is stored in its own directory.
168	
169	```
170	data/house/
171	  2022_Registrations_XML/
172	    *.xml
173	  2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
174	  2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
175	  2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
176	  2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
177	  2026_Registrations_XML/
178	  2026_1stQuarter_XML/
179	```
180	
181	Root element is `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE1>` (registrations) or `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE2>` (quarterlies). Filenames are numeric House filing IDs (e.g. `301642857.xml`).
182	
183	**Key fields.** `organizationName`, `clientName`, `senateID`, `houseID`, `lobbyists/lobbyist[]` (with `coveredPosition`, `lobbyistNew`), `alis/ali_Code[]` (ALI issue codes — up to 9 slots, often sparse), `specific_issues/description` (free text, often naming specific bills and agencies), `income`/`expenses`, `governmentEntities`, `foreignEntities`.
184	
185	**Source.** [House Clerk Lobbying Disclosure](https://disclosurespreview.house.gov/).
186	
187	**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 = Q1 + registrations).
188	
189	**Load it.**
190	
191	```python
192	from pathlib import Path
193	import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
194	
195	path = Path("data/house/2025_1stQuarter_XML")
196	for xml_path in sorted(path.glob("*.xml"))[:5]:
197	    root = ET.parse(xml_path).getroot()
198	    org = root.findtext("organizationName", "").strip()
199	    client = root.findtext("clientName", "").strip()
200	    issues = [a.text for a in root.findall(".//alis/ali_Code") if (a.text or "").strip()]
201	    print(xml_path.name, "|", org, "→", client, "|", issues)
202	```
203	
204	Expect a fair amount of whitespace-only text inside elements; `.strip()` everything. Many quarterly filings carry the same `senateID` as a Senate filing — that's the bridge between the two lobbying datasets.
205	
206	**Starting points.**
207	- Senate↔House reconciliation: fuzzy-match on `organizationName`/`clientName` and validate with the shared `senateID`/`houseID`. Discrepancies between the two filings for the same engagement are newsworthy.
208	- Bill-level lobbying maps: `specific_issues/description` often names bill numbers (`H.R. 1234`) — extract and join against Congress.gov bill and vote data.
209	- Entity-resolution challenge: the House data has no UUIDs and no standardized casing; building a clean entity table across Senate + House is itself a deliverable.
210	
211	---
212	
213	## Cross-dataset leads
214	
215	The highest-value questions require more than one of these:
216	
217	- **"Say vs. pay":** For a member (keyed by `bioguide_id` in the press corpus), correlate press-release topics with lobbying activity targeting their chamber/committee around the same quarter.
218	- **Temporal lobbying–messaging coupling:** When quarterly lobbying spend on an issue spikes, does the language in press releases shift? Good test for causal-adjacent narrative work.
219	- **Entity graph:** Press-release NER → companies/orgs → Senate/House LDA registrants & clients → government entities lobbied → committees → members. The cross-walks are the investigation.
220	
221	## Useful external enrichments
222	
223	- [Congress.gov bulk data / API](https://www.congress.gov/help/using-data-offsite) — bills, votes, committee assignments, cosponsorships. Joins via `bioguide_id`.
224	- [FEC](https://www.fec.gov/data/) — campaign finance to ground-truth lobbyist contributions.
225	- [FARA](https://efile.fara.gov/ords/fara/r/fara_ws/api/bulkdata) — foreign-agent filings, complements the `foreign_entities` fields.
226	- [Federal Register](https://www.federalregister.gov/developers/api/v1) — regulatory outcomes of lobbying on specific rules.
227

# Data Manual

This dataset is stored under `data/`. Together the files cover congressional communications and the lobbying activity aimed at Congress from 2022 through Q1 2026. This manual describes the current on-disk layout, how to load the data, and where to start digging.

## Data layout

The `data/` directory is decompressed.

```
data/
  congress_press/
    2022/2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
    2023/2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
    2024/2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
    2025/2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
    2026-01.jsonl
    2026-02.jsonl
    2026-03.jsonl
  senate/
    2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
    constants/
  house/
    2022_Registrations_XML/
    2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
    2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
    2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
    2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
    2026_Registrations_XML/
    2026_1stQuarter_XML/
```

## At a glance

| Dataset | Path | Format | Coverage | On-disk size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Congress press releases | `data/congress_press/` | JSONL (51 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026-03 | 504 MB |
| Senate LDA filings & contributions | `data/senate/` | JSON (arrays, nested) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 2.2 GB |
| House LDA registrations & reports | `data/house/` | XML (409,650 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 5.9 GB |

All three are public records. Expect self-reported data, missing fields, and inconsistent conventions — part of the investigative work is reconciling them.

---

## 1. Congress press releases — `data/congress_press/`

**Description.** Press releases scraped from the official `*.house.gov` and `*.senate.gov` member websites. Each record is one release with light member metadata and the full body text.

**Scale.** ~48K releases in 2025 alone (≈478 members represented); the full 2022–2026-Q1 corpus runs larger. Average release ~900 words → tens of millions of words of unstructured text.

**Format.** Newline-delimited JSON (JSONL). One record per line. The full years 2022 through 2025 are split into year directories; the 2026 Q1 files sit directly in `data/congress_press/`.

```
data/congress_press/
  2022/
    2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
  2023/
    2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
  2024/
    2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
  2025/
    2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
  2026-01.jsonl
  2026-02.jsonl
  2026-03.jsonl
```

**Record fields.** `url`, `title`, `date`, `date_source`, `source` (member press-release index page), `domain`, `scraper` (scraper ID), `member` (`bioguide_id`, `name`, `party`, `state`, `chamber`), `text` (full body, newline-preserved).

**Source.** [Congress Press](https://thescoop.org/congress-press/)

**Timeframe.** 2022-01 through 2026-03. The 2026 files currently include January through March at the root of `data/congress_press/`.

**Load it.**

```python
import json
from pathlib import Path

root = Path("data/congress_press")

# Single month
with open(root / "2026-01.jsonl") as f:
    for line in f:
        rec = json.loads(line)
        print(rec["member"]["name"], rec["title"])

# Full decompressed year
for path in sorted((root / "2025").glob("*.jsonl")):
    with open(path) as f:
        for line in f:
            rec = json.loads(line)
            # ...
```

**Starting points.**
- Keyword and frequency analysis: who talks about a given issue, how often, and when does the language shift.
- Comparative messaging: same event, different party framing — pull all releases from a +/- 7-day window around a news event and cluster.
- Member activity baselines: releases/month per member; outliers often correlate with primary campaigns, committee news, or scandal cycles.
- Entity extraction: NER over `text` to pull companies, agencies, and bills — the output bridges naturally to the lobbying datasets via `bioguide_id` and entity names.

---

## 2. Senate LDA data — `data/senate/`

**Description.** Lobbying Disclosure Act filings submitted to the Secretary of the Senate: registrations, quarterly activity reports, and semiannual contribution (LD-203) reports. This is the "who is lobbying whom, on what, for how much" dataset.

**Scale (2025).** 108,225 filings, 39,438 contribution reports (with ~149K line items across them), 5,470 registrants, 28,246 clients. Earlier years are comparable.

**Format.** JSON arrays, deeply nested. Each filing includes its registrant, client, lobbying activities (with ALI issue codes and lobbyists), and government entities lobbied. Contribution reports include a `pacs` and `contribution_items` list.

```
data/senate/
  2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
    filings/filings_{year}.json          # array of filing objects
    contributions/contributions_{year}.json
  constants/
    countries.json
    filing_types.json                    # RR, Q1..Q4, MM (mid-year), YE (year-end), etc.
    government_entities.json             # agencies/chambers lobbied
    lobbying_activity_issues.json        # 3-letter ALI codes (BUD, TAX, HCR, …)
    states.json
    lobbyist_prefixes.json
    lobbyist_suffixes.json
    contribution_item_types.json
```

**Key fields.** Filing: `filing_uuid`, `filing_type`, `filing_period`, `filing_year`, `income`, `expenses`, `registrant` (nested), `client` (nested), `lobbying_activities[]` (each with `general_issue_code`, `description`, `lobbyists[]`, `government_entities[]`). Contributions: `filing_uuid`, `filer_type`, `registrant`, `lobbyist`, `pacs[]`, `contribution_items[]` (with `type`, `amount`, `payee`, `honoree`, `contributor_name`).

**Source.** [Senate LDA API](https://lda.senate.gov/api/v1/) (`filings`, `contributions` endpoints).

**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 is Q1 only).

**Load it.**

```python
import json

with open("data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json") as f:
    filings = json.load(f)           # list of dicts

print(len(filings), "filings")
f0 = filings[0]
print(f0["registrant"]["name"], "→", f0["client"]["name"])
for act in f0["lobbying_activities"]:
    print(" ", act["general_issue_code"], act["description"][:80])

# Decode issue codes using the constants file
issues = {c["value"]: c["name"]
          for c in json.load(open("data/senate/constants/lobbying_activity_issues.json"))}
```

**Starting points.**
- Top-of-funnel aggregates: biggest spenders by client, most-active registrants, busiest issue codes per quarter.
- Revolving door: the `covered_position` field on each lobbyist flags prior government roles — mine it for text patterns ("Chief of Staff to Sen. …").
- Foreign influence: filter on `foreign_entities[]` across filings; cross-reference with FARA.
- Contribution flows: `contribution_items[].payee` vs. `honoree` reveals who lobbyists and their PACs are funding. Join to FEC for validation.
- Data-quality stories: Many filings lack income data, expenses, and state info. The gaps are themselves reportable.

---

## 3. House LDA data — `data/house/`

**Description.** The House Clerk's parallel set of LDA disclosures. Registrations (LD-1) and quarterly activity reports (LD-2). Same underlying disclosure regime as the Senate data, different distribution format and slightly different schema.

**Scale (2025).** ~7,762 registrations + ~100,760 quarterly records ≈ 108K records, comparable to the Senate side. The full extracted House tree contains 409,650 XML files.

**Format.** One XML document per filing. Each reporting period is stored in its own directory.

```
data/house/
  2022_Registrations_XML/
    *.xml
  2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
  2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
  2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
  2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
  2026_Registrations_XML/
  2026_1stQuarter_XML/
```

Root element is `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE1>` (registrations) or `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE2>` (quarterlies). Filenames are numeric House filing IDs (e.g. `301642857.xml`).

**Key fields.** `organizationName`, `clientName`, `senateID`, `houseID`, `lobbyists/lobbyist[]` (with `coveredPosition`, `lobbyistNew`), `alis/ali_Code[]` (ALI issue codes — up to 9 slots, often sparse), `specific_issues/description` (free text, often naming specific bills and agencies), `income`/`expenses`, `governmentEntities`, `foreignEntities`.

**Source.** [House Clerk Lobbying Disclosure](https://disclosurespreview.house.gov/).

**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 = Q1 + registrations).

**Load it.**

```python
from pathlib import Path
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

path = Path("data/house/2025_1stQuarter_XML")
for xml_path in sorted(path.glob("*.xml"))[:5]:
    root = ET.parse(xml_path).getroot()
    org = root.findtext("organizationName", "").strip()
    client = root.findtext("clientName", "").strip()
    issues = [a.text for a in root.findall(".//alis/ali_Code") if (a.text or "").strip()]
    print(xml_path.name, "|", org, "→", client, "|", issues)
```

Expect a fair amount of whitespace-only text inside elements; `.strip()` everything. Many quarterly filings carry the same `senateID` as a Senate filing — that's the bridge between the two lobbying datasets.

**Starting points.**
- Senate↔House reconciliation: fuzzy-match on `organizationName`/`clientName` and validate with the shared `senateID`/`houseID`. Discrepancies between the two filings for the same engagement are newsworthy.
- Bill-level lobbying maps: `specific_issues/description` often names bill numbers (`H.R. 1234`) — extract and join against Congress.gov bill and vote data.
- Entity-resolution challenge: the House data has no UUIDs and no standardized casing; building a clean entity table across Senate + House is itself a deliverable.

---

## Cross-dataset leads

The highest-value questions require more than one of these:

- **"Say vs. pay":** For a member (keyed by `bioguide_id` in the press corpus), correlate press-release topics with lobbying activity targeting their chamber/committee around the same quarter.
- **Temporal lobbying–messaging coupling:** When quarterly lobbying spend on an issue spikes, does the language in press releases shift? Good test for causal-adjacent narrative work.
- **Entity graph:** Press-release NER → companies/orgs → Senate/House LDA registrants & clients → government entities lobbied → committees → members. The cross-walks are the investigation.

## Useful external enrichments

- [Congress.gov bulk data / API](https://www.congress.gov/help/using-data-offsite) — bills, votes, committee assignments, cosponsorships. Joins via `bioguide_id`.
- [FEC](https://www.fec.gov/data/) — campaign finance to ground-truth lobbyist contributions.
- [FARA](https://efile.fara.gov/ords/fara/r/fara_ws/api/bulkdata) — foreign-agent filings, complements the `foreign_entities` fields.
- [Federal Register](https://www.federalregister.gov/developers/api/v1) — regulatory outcomes of lobbying on specific rules.

1	# Project — Market Trends Research
2	
3	A curated corpus of healthcare-marketing summaries — webinars and podcasts — used by Rob Calvey (Loyal Health) to inform Digital Front Door (DFD) product strategy. Each `.md` file in `Webinars/` represents one source (the folder name is historical — it holds both webinars and podcasts). The structure: a Summary, an executive-relevance takeaway (`ELT Summary` on webinars, `Strategic Summary: Healthcare Marketing Signals` on podcasts — Loyal Health Executive Leadership Team is the audience for both), the full transcript, and a YAML metadata block at the end for tagging.
4	
5	## To add new files
6	
7	Follow [`_Workflow.md`](_Workflow.md) — the canonical intake checklist. It branches by source: ehealthcareStrategy webinars use the Phase 1 Chrome widget; pre-produced summaries (e.g., Hedy & Hopp podcasts) skip Phase 1 and start at Phase 2.
8	
9	## Repo layout
10	
11	```
12	Market Trends Research/
13	├── CLAUDE.md                                        ← this file
14	├── _Workflow.md                                     ← monthly intake checklist (start here)
15	├── .claude/skills/clean-transcripts/SKILL.md        ← Phase 3 cleaning skill (project-scoped)
16	├── Webinars/                                        ← the corpus + intake/process files (name historical — holds webinars + podcasts)
17	│   ├── YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md                         ← the corpus (~144 files and growing — webinars + podcasts)
18	│   ├── _Topic-Taxonomy.md                           ← canonical tag vocabulary
19	│   ├── _Tagging-Prompt.md                           ← Phase 5 (tagging) prompt
20	│   ├── _metadata_parser.py                          ← shared YAML parser (used by both Phase 6 scripts)
21	│   ├── _generate-index.py                           ← Phase 6 index regenerator
22	│   ├── _Topic-Index.md                              ← auto-generated overview (per-category teasers + DFD teaser + time cohorts + publisher + grade roll-up)
23	│   ├── _Index-<Category>.md                         ← auto-generated per-category tag → files lookup (one file per taxonomy category present in the data)
24	│   ├── _Index-DFD.md                                ← auto-generated DFD product → files lookup (the load-bearing synthesis lookup)
25	│   ├── _generate-scan.py                            ← Phase 6 scan regenerator
26	│   ├── _Scan.md                                     ← auto-generated breadth-scan extract (Summary + Strategic/ELT per file); navigation-only, never a citation source
27	│   ├── _Audit.md                                    ← summary-fidelity grades (A–F)
28	│   ├── _Grading-Calibration.md                      ← preserved bad summaries (grading anchors)
29	│   ├── Transcripts to clean/                        ← staging for new batches
30	│   └── Summary-Creation-Workflows/                  ← per-source intake prompts + transcription tooling
31	│       ├── _process-new-ehealthcare-strategy-webinars.md    ← Phase 1 (Chrome widget) prompt
32	│       ├── _process-new-hedy-and-hopp-podcast.md            ← Hedy & Hopp processing prompt
33	│       ├── _process-new-touchpoint-podcast.md               ← Touch Point processing prompt
34	│       ├── _webinar_summary_audit_prompt.md                 ← Phase 4 (audit) prompt template
35	│       ├── _transcription-lessons.md                        ← accumulated transcription/diarization lessons
36	│       ├── Transcript_Generation.py                         ← Whisper + PyAnnote pipeline (Touch Point) — runs in Colab
37	│       ├── _sync_to_github.sh / _sync_from_github.sh        ← bidirectional sync between local .py and Colab .ipynb (see Conventions)
38	│       ├── _sync_to_github.py / _sync_from_github.py        ← Python implementations called by the wrappers
39	│       ├── _sync_common.py                                  ← shared utilities (keychain, GitHub API, jupytext, diff)
40	│       └── .venv-sync/                                      ← Python venv with jupytext (created by sync setup; ignore)
41	└── Analysis/                                        ← synthesis outputs (VoC matrices, validations, ELT briefs)
42	    ├── _Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md         ← workflow for periodic market-trends/VoC synthesis
43	    │                                                  (future siblings: _Vendor-Profile-Workflow.md, _Targeted-Query-Workflow.md, etc.)
44	    └── _VoC-<YYYY>-<HX>.md                          ← periodic voice-of-customer snapshots
45	```
46	
47	## Conventions
48	
49	- **Filenames**: dated corpus files are `YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md` and live in `Webinars/`. Anything starting with `_` is a process/support/synthesis file, never a corpus entry.
50	- **Analysis filenames**: synthesis outputs in `Analysis/` follow `_<Type>-<Identifier>.md`:
51	  - `_VoC-YYYY-HX.md` — periodic voice-of-customer snapshots (e.g., `_VoC-2026-H2.md`)
52	  - `_themes-YYYY-HX.md` — themes-only extract from the corresponding `_VoC-YYYY-HX.md`, used as a symlink target by downstream artifacts (positioning, briefs, sales messaging). Regenerated via the awk command in the synthesis workflow's Phase 5 Step 5g whenever the parent VoC changes. Do not edit directly.
53	  - `_Validation-PN-<slug>.md` — per-problem validation outputs (e.g., `_Validation-P1-Identity-Resolution.md`)
54	  - `_Brief-<topic>-YYYY-MM-DD.md` — ad-hoc analysis briefs for specific questions
55	  - `_ELT-<topic>-YYYY-MM-DD.md` — anything synthesized for ELT consumption
56	  Date-stamped so re-runs don't overwrite history.
57	- **Tooling colocation**: workflow tooling lives alongside the artifact it produces — per-source intake prompts and pipeline scripts in `Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/` (produces `Webinars/` corpus files), synthesis workflow in `Analysis/` (produces `Analysis/` outputs). Don't add new process `.md` files or scripts at the project root; only `CLAUDE.md` and `_Workflow.md` (the cross-folder orchestrator) belong there.
58	- **Tags**: every corpus file has a `## Metadata` block at the end with YAML — including `format` (`webinar`|`podcast`), `publisher`, `primary_topics`, `secondary_topics`, `tags.<category>`, `quality_grade`, `dfd_relevance`. Tags must use canonical forms from `Webinars/_Topic-Taxonomy.md`. See [`Webinars/_Tagging-Prompt.md`](Webinars/_Tagging-Prompt.md) for full rules.
59	- **DFD product names**: PDM, Provider Search, Provider Scheduling, Listing Management, Review Boost, HRA, CRM. Use these exact strings (not "Online Scheduling," not "3rd Party Listings," etc.) in any new content.
60	- **Audit grades**: A = accurate, F = describes a webinar/episode that didn't happen. Files graded C or D need verification before being cited to ELT. The current grade is the bold letter outside parens (e.g., `**A** (was D)` means current grade is A).
61	- **Audit banners**: Every non-A file has a one-line audit banner below its header block (Source / Date / Slides for webinars; Source / Date / Host / Guest(s) / Description for podcasts). If you see a banner, consult `Webinars/_Audit.md` before citing. YAML `quality_grade` is the machine-readable source of truth; the banner is the human-skim signal. A files have no banner by design.
62	- **External-source annotations in synthesis**: when a synthesis theme needs to reference context that isn't from the corpus (external research, vendor articles, law-firm overviews, web inspection), use a trailing annotation after the theme's source blocks in the form `— External reference (non-corpus, YYYY-MM-DD):` or `— External web research (non-corpus, YYYY-MM-DD):`. Body describes what's referenced and explicitly flags it as outside the corpus. Examples in [Analysis/_VoC-2026-H2.md](Analysis/_VoC-2026-H2.md) at Themes 3 (web research on Children's Nebraska stack) and 4 (law-firm overview of January 2026 state privacy laws).
63	- **Index regeneration**: run `python3 Webinars/_generate-index.py` after any change that affects tags. Emits the slim overview `_Topic-Index.md`, one `_Index-<Category>.md` per taxonomy category found in the data (Vendors, Concepts, Tactics, Tech, Regulatory, Service Lines, Populations), and `_Index-DFD.md` for DFD product → files (the load-bearing synthesis lookup). Categories are discovered from the YAML — adding a new category to the taxonomy emits a new file on next regen without code changes. Counts use the format `total · format-mix · grade-mix` (e.g., `38 · 22w/16p · 30A 6B 2C`) so voice mix and trust mix are visible inline. No external dependencies — uses the shared `_metadata_parser.py` module.
64	- **Scan regeneration**: run `python3 Webinars/_generate-scan.py` after any change that adds files or edits Summary/Strategic/ELT sections. Produces `Webinars/_Scan.md`, a breadth-scan extract of every file's two summary sections sorted newest-first. `_Scan.md` is **navigation-only** — never a citation source. The preamble in the file restates the rule for any agent that reads it.
65	- **Deletes use `trash`, not `rm`**: file deletion in this project goes through the `trash` CLI (macOS Trash) so artifacts are recoverable. `.claude/settings.local.json` denies `Bash(rm *)` and `Bash(rmdir *)`. Origin: 2026-06-04 incident — 2.3 GB of intermediate Whisper/PyAnnote audio lost after `rm` ran directly instead of going to Trash. `trash` is at `/opt/homebrew/opt/trash/bin/trash` (Homebrew keg-only) and on PATH via `~/.zshrc`.
66	- **Notebook sync (Transcript_Generation.py ↔ Colab)**: `Transcript_Generation.py` runs in Google Colab as `Transcription.ipynb` at `robcalveyloyal/Transcription`. The `.py` is canonical; bidirectional sync via two scripts in `Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/`:
67	  - `./Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/_sync_to_github.sh` — push local `.py` → remote `.ipynb` (run after editing locally).
68	  - `./Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/_sync_from_github.sh` — pull remote `.ipynb` → local `.py` (run after editing in Colab).
69	  Both scripts safety-check for divergence on the opposite side using `.last_sync_sha` state — if the side you're about to overwrite has changed since the last sync, the script shows a diff and prompts. Use `--force` to skip the prompt. PAT lives in macOS Keychain (service `github-transcription-sync`). `# %%` markers in the `.py` produce a 2-cell notebook (cell 1: pip installs, run once per Colab session; cell 2: everything else).
70	- **GitHub backup**: project mirrors to private repo `robcalveyloyal/market-trends-research`. Push after each intake batch (see `_Workflow.md` Phase 7) and each synthesis output (see `Analysis/_Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md` Mode A Step 5h / Mode B Step 10). Auth is `gh`'s OAuth token in the macOS Keychain — `git push` over HTTPS just works. Separate from `github-transcription-sync` (the purpose-built fine-grained PAT used by the notebook-sync scripts above for the `robcalveyloyal/Transcription` repo); don't conflate the two.
71	
72	## When you're helping with this project
73	
74	- For routine intake work, follow `_Workflow.md` phase-by-phase. Each phase has explicit "what good looks like" checks.
75	- For tagging or topic synthesis questions, consult `Webinars/_Topic-Taxonomy.md` first — never invent a tag string without checking.
76	- For trend analysis questions ("which files discussed X?"), start at `Webinars/_Topic-Index.md` (overview with per-category teasers + DFD teaser + half-year cohorts + by-publisher + grade roll-up). Click through to the relevant `_Index-<Category>.md` for the full tag → files list, or `_Index-DFD.md` for the DFD product → files list. Both before grepping.
77	- For time-bounded trend questions ("what shifted in H1 2026?"), check the **By time cohort** section in `Webinars/_Topic-Index.md` for per-period file counts and voice/trust mix. For the file list within a cohort, scan `_Scan.md` (sorted newest-first) and stop when you exit the date range, or use a filename glob (e.g., `Webinars/2026-0[1-6]-*.md` for 2026 H1).
78	- For breadth-scan questions ("what does the corpus collectively say about X?"), `Webinars/_Scan.md` is the entry-point — every file's Summary + Strategic/ELT in one place. Do not cite from it: load source files for any quote or claim that lands in synthesis output. At scale, prefer spawning a scanner sub-agent that reads `_Scan.md` and returns a digest of relevant filenames (see [Analysis/_Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md](Analysis/_Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md) for the pattern) rather than loading the file into the main session.
79	- For fidelity-sensitive synthesis (anything heading to ELT or sales conversations), check `quality_grade` in each file's metadata and down-weight C/D files.
80	- For analysis/synthesis work (VoC matrices, problem validations, ELT briefs), outputs live in `Analysis/`. Check existing files there before producing new ones — synthesis is layered on top of the corpus, not duplicated alongside it.
81

# Project — Market Trends Research

A curated corpus of healthcare-marketing summaries — webinars and podcasts — used by Rob Calvey (Loyal Health) to inform Digital Front Door (DFD) product strategy. Each `.md` file in `Webinars/` represents one source (the folder name is historical — it holds both webinars and podcasts). The structure: a Summary, an executive-relevance takeaway (`ELT Summary` on webinars, `Strategic Summary: Healthcare Marketing Signals` on podcasts — Loyal Health Executive Leadership Team is the audience for both), the full transcript, and a YAML metadata block at the end for tagging.

## To add new files

Follow [`_Workflow.md`](_Workflow.md) — the canonical intake checklist. It branches by source: ehealthcareStrategy webinars use the Phase 1 Chrome widget; pre-produced summaries (e.g., Hedy & Hopp podcasts) skip Phase 1 and start at Phase 2.

## Repo layout

```
Market Trends Research/
├── CLAUDE.md                                        ← this file
├── _Workflow.md                                     ← monthly intake checklist (start here)
├── .claude/skills/clean-transcripts/SKILL.md        ← Phase 3 cleaning skill (project-scoped)
├── Webinars/                                        ← the corpus + intake/process files (name historical — holds webinars + podcasts)
│   ├── YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md                         ← the corpus (~144 files and growing — webinars + podcasts)
│   ├── _Topic-Taxonomy.md                           ← canonical tag vocabulary
│   ├── _Tagging-Prompt.md                           ← Phase 5 (tagging) prompt
│   ├── _metadata_parser.py                          ← shared YAML parser (used by both Phase 6 scripts)
│   ├── _generate-index.py                           ← Phase 6 index regenerator
│   ├── _Topic-Index.md                              ← auto-generated overview (per-category teasers + DFD teaser + time cohorts + publisher + grade roll-up)
│   ├── _Index-<Category>.md                         ← auto-generated per-category tag → files lookup (one file per taxonomy category present in the data)
│   ├── _Index-DFD.md                                ← auto-generated DFD product → files lookup (the load-bearing synthesis lookup)
│   ├── _generate-scan.py                            ← Phase 6 scan regenerator
│   ├── _Scan.md                                     ← auto-generated breadth-scan extract (Summary + Strategic/ELT per file); navigation-only, never a citation source
│   ├── _Audit.md                                    ← summary-fidelity grades (A–F)
│   ├── _Grading-Calibration.md                      ← preserved bad summaries (grading anchors)
│   ├── Transcripts to clean/                        ← staging for new batches
│   └── Summary-Creation-Workflows/                  ← per-source intake prompts + transcription tooling
│       ├── _process-new-ehealthcare-strategy-webinars.md    ← Phase 1 (Chrome widget) prompt
│       ├── _process-new-hedy-and-hopp-podcast.md            ← Hedy & Hopp processing prompt
│       ├── _process-new-touchpoint-podcast.md               ← Touch Point processing prompt
│       ├── _webinar_summary_audit_prompt.md                 ← Phase 4 (audit) prompt template
│       ├── _transcription-lessons.md                        ← accumulated transcription/diarization lessons
│       ├── Transcript_Generation.py                         ← Whisper + PyAnnote pipeline (Touch Point) — runs in Colab
│       ├── _sync_to_github.sh / _sync_from_github.sh        ← bidirectional sync between local .py and Colab .ipynb (see Conventions)
│       ├── _sync_to_github.py / _sync_from_github.py        ← Python implementations called by the wrappers
│       ├── _sync_common.py                                  ← shared utilities (keychain, GitHub API, jupytext, diff)
│       └── .venv-sync/                                      ← Python venv with jupytext (created by sync setup; ignore)
└── Analysis/                                        ← synthesis outputs (VoC matrices, validations, ELT briefs)
    ├── _Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md         ← workflow for periodic market-trends/VoC synthesis
    │                                                  (future siblings: _Vendor-Profile-Workflow.md, _Targeted-Query-Workflow.md, etc.)
    └── _VoC-<YYYY>-<HX>.md                          ← periodic voice-of-customer snapshots
```

## Conventions

- **Filenames**: dated corpus files are `YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md` and live in `Webinars/`. Anything starting with `_` is a process/support/synthesis file, never a corpus entry.
- **Analysis filenames**: synthesis outputs in `Analysis/` follow `_<Type>-<Identifier>.md`:
  - `_VoC-YYYY-HX.md` — periodic voice-of-customer snapshots (e.g., `_VoC-2026-H2.md`)
  - `_themes-YYYY-HX.md` — themes-only extract from the corresponding `_VoC-YYYY-HX.md`, used as a symlink target by downstream artifacts (positioning, briefs, sales messaging). Regenerated via the awk command in the synthesis workflow's Phase 5 Step 5g whenever the parent VoC changes. Do not edit directly.
  - `_Validation-PN-<slug>.md` — per-problem validation outputs (e.g., `_Validation-P1-Identity-Resolution.md`)
  - `_Brief-<topic>-YYYY-MM-DD.md` — ad-hoc analysis briefs for specific questions
  - `_ELT-<topic>-YYYY-MM-DD.md` — anything synthesized for ELT consumption
  Date-stamped so re-runs don't overwrite history.
- **Tooling colocation**: workflow tooling lives alongside the artifact it produces — per-source intake prompts and pipeline scripts in `Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/` (produces `Webinars/` corpus files), synthesis workflow in `Analysis/` (produces `Analysis/` outputs). Don't add new process `.md` files or scripts at the project root; only `CLAUDE.md` and `_Workflow.md` (the cross-folder orchestrator) belong there.
- **Tags**: every corpus file has a `## Metadata` block at the end with YAML — including `format` (`webinar`|`podcast`), `publisher`, `primary_topics`, `secondary_topics`, `tags.<category>`, `quality_grade`, `dfd_relevance`. Tags must use canonical forms from `Webinars/_Topic-Taxonomy.md`. See [`Webinars/_Tagging-Prompt.md`](Webinars/_Tagging-Prompt.md) for full rules.
- **DFD product names**: PDM, Provider Search, Provider Scheduling, Listing Management, Review Boost, HRA, CRM. Use these exact strings (not "Online Scheduling," not "3rd Party Listings," etc.) in any new content.
- **Audit grades**: A = accurate, F = describes a webinar/episode that didn't happen. Files graded C or D need verification before being cited to ELT. The current grade is the bold letter outside parens (e.g., `**A** (was D)` means current grade is A).
- **Audit banners**: Every non-A file has a one-line audit banner below its header block (Source / Date / Slides for webinars; Source / Date / Host / Guest(s) / Description for podcasts). If you see a banner, consult `Webinars/_Audit.md` before citing. YAML `quality_grade` is the machine-readable source of truth; the banner is the human-skim signal. A files have no banner by design.
- **External-source annotations in synthesis**: when a synthesis theme needs to reference context that isn't from the corpus (external research, vendor articles, law-firm overviews, web inspection), use a trailing annotation after the theme's source blocks in the form `— External reference (non-corpus, YYYY-MM-DD):` or `— External web research (non-corpus, YYYY-MM-DD):`. Body describes what's referenced and explicitly flags it as outside the corpus. Examples in [Analysis/_VoC-2026-H2.md](Analysis/_VoC-2026-H2.md) at Themes 3 (web research on Children's Nebraska stack) and 4 (law-firm overview of January 2026 state privacy laws).
- **Index regeneration**: run `python3 Webinars/_generate-index.py` after any change that affects tags. Emits the slim overview `_Topic-Index.md`, one `_Index-<Category>.md` per taxonomy category found in the data (Vendors, Concepts, Tactics, Tech, Regulatory, Service Lines, Populations), and `_Index-DFD.md` for DFD product → files (the load-bearing synthesis lookup). Categories are discovered from the YAML — adding a new category to the taxonomy emits a new file on next regen without code changes. Counts use the format `total · format-mix · grade-mix` (e.g., `38 · 22w/16p · 30A 6B 2C`) so voice mix and trust mix are visible inline. No external dependencies — uses the shared `_metadata_parser.py` module.
- **Scan regeneration**: run `python3 Webinars/_generate-scan.py` after any change that adds files or edits Summary/Strategic/ELT sections. Produces `Webinars/_Scan.md`, a breadth-scan extract of every file's two summary sections sorted newest-first. `_Scan.md` is **navigation-only** — never a citation source. The preamble in the file restates the rule for any agent that reads it.
- **Deletes use `trash`, not `rm`**: file deletion in this project goes through the `trash` CLI (macOS Trash) so artifacts are recoverable. `.claude/settings.local.json` denies `Bash(rm *)` and `Bash(rmdir *)`. Origin: 2026-06-04 incident — 2.3 GB of intermediate Whisper/PyAnnote audio lost after `rm` ran directly instead of going to Trash. `trash` is at `/opt/homebrew/opt/trash/bin/trash` (Homebrew keg-only) and on PATH via `~/.zshrc`.
- **Notebook sync (Transcript_Generation.py ↔ Colab)**: `Transcript_Generation.py` runs in Google Colab as `Transcription.ipynb` at `robcalveyloyal/Transcription`. The `.py` is canonical; bidirectional sync via two scripts in `Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/`:
  - `./Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/_sync_to_github.sh` — push local `.py` → remote `.ipynb` (run after editing locally).
  - `./Webinars/Summary-Creation-Workflows/_sync_from_github.sh` — pull remote `.ipynb` → local `.py` (run after editing in Colab).
  Both scripts safety-check for divergence on the opposite side using `.last_sync_sha` state — if the side you're about to overwrite has changed since the last sync, the script shows a diff and prompts. Use `--force` to skip the prompt. PAT lives in macOS Keychain (service `github-transcription-sync`). `# %%` markers in the `.py` produce a 2-cell notebook (cell 1: pip installs, run once per Colab session; cell 2: everything else).
- **GitHub backup**: project mirrors to private repo `robcalveyloyal/market-trends-research`. Push after each intake batch (see `_Workflow.md` Phase 7) and each synthesis output (see `Analysis/_Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md` Mode A Step 5h / Mode B Step 10). Auth is `gh`'s OAuth token in the macOS Keychain — `git push` over HTTPS just works. Separate from `github-transcription-sync` (the purpose-built fine-grained PAT used by the notebook-sync scripts above for the `robcalveyloyal/Transcription` repo); don't conflate the two.

## When you're helping with this project

- For routine intake work, follow `_Workflow.md` phase-by-phase. Each phase has explicit "what good looks like" checks.
- For tagging or topic synthesis questions, consult `Webinars/_Topic-Taxonomy.md` first — never invent a tag string without checking.
- For trend analysis questions ("which files discussed X?"), start at `Webinars/_Topic-Index.md` (overview with per-category teasers + DFD teaser + half-year cohorts + by-publisher + grade roll-up). Click through to the relevant `_Index-<Category>.md` for the full tag → files list, or `_Index-DFD.md` for the DFD product → files list. Both before grepping.
- For time-bounded trend questions ("what shifted in H1 2026?"), check the **By time cohort** section in `Webinars/_Topic-Index.md` for per-period file counts and voice/trust mix. For the file list within a cohort, scan `_Scan.md` (sorted newest-first) and stop when you exit the date range, or use a filename glob (e.g., `Webinars/2026-0[1-6]-*.md` for 2026 H1).
- For breadth-scan questions ("what does the corpus collectively say about X?"), `Webinars/_Scan.md` is the entry-point — every file's Summary + Strategic/ELT in one place. Do not cite from it: load source files for any quote or claim that lands in synthesis output. At scale, prefer spawning a scanner sub-agent that reads `_Scan.md` and returns a digest of relevant filenames (see [Analysis/_Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md](Analysis/_Market-Trends-Synthesis-Workflow.md) for the pattern) rather than loading the file into the main session.
- For fidelity-sensitive synthesis (anything heading to ELT or sales conversations), check `quality_grade` in each file's metadata and down-weight C/D files.
- For analysis/synthesis work (VoC matrices, problem validations, ELT briefs), outputs live in `Analysis/`. Check existing files there before producing new ones — synthesis is layered on top of the corpus, not duplicated alongside it.
